title: Global Bounds beyond Local Quantum Metrology
abstract: Quantum Cramér--Rao theory is intrinsically local: it bounds precision near a specified parameter value, and its saturating measurement generally depends on that value. Barankin-type bounds use finite parameter displacements, but remain anchored to a chosen reference value. This leaves open a basic global-estimation problem: when the parameter is known only within a broad domain, what precision can be guaranteed by a single estimator and a single measurement strategy fixed before the true value is localized? We answer this question by introducing global score functions tied to a weighted variance over the whole parameter domain. Their correlations generate a hierarchy of precision bounds: global Cramér--Rao and Barankin-type bounds arise as restricted levels, whereas unrestricted score correlations yield a fully global bound for the prescribed weighted variance. The hierarchy recovers local Cramér--Rao theory in the many-repetition limit and reveals genuinely global precision limits for finite data over broad domains. In the quantum setting, the construction identifies when this fully global bound can be realized by a single parameter-independent measurement. The same framework extends to Bayesian estimation, recovering the Van Trees bound in the local limit while yielding stronger finite-width lower bounds on the Bayesian mean-square error beyond this limit.
summary: The paper develops a new hierarchy of precision bounds for quantum metrology that applies to global parameter estimation rather than just local precision. It demonstrates that when a parameter is known only within a broad range, a single fixed measurement strategy can achieve a fundamental 'fully global' limit. This framework also provides a unified approach to strengthening Bayesian estimation bounds.
main_problem: The paper addresses the limitation of local quantum metrology, which only bounds precision near a specific parameter value, by investigating the fundamental precision limits for global parameter estimation over a broad domain.
main_result: The authors establish a hierarchy of precision bounds (Global Cramér-Rao, Global Barankin, and Fully Global) and show that a single, parameter-independent measurement can saturate the fully global bound in certain quantum models.
method: The authors introduce global score functions tied to a weighted variance and use a hierarchy matrix to couple score directions across the parameter domain, applying the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality and the Rayleigh quotient maximization theorem.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to general quantum statistical models, including a noisy qubit model with a density operator parameterized by an angle, and a classical binary measurement model.
key_observables: Weighted variance of the estimator, Bayesian mean-square error (BMSE), and the quantum information matrix.
important_parameters: Number of measurement repetitions (m), parameter domain density (n), parameter domain width, and the bandwidth (epsilon) of the Bayesian hierarchy kernel.
main_assumptions: Assumes a discrete set of parameter values for the frequentist derivation and assumes the probability distribution is strictly positive for all outcomes.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows the generalized Bayesian bound as a function of the error parameter epsilon.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the global estimation problem, introduces the global score function and the hierarchy of bounds, provides the mathematical derivation for classical and quantum settings, demonstrates saturation conditions and optimal measurement design, and extends the framework to Bayesian estimation.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for quantum metrology and quantum optics, as it provides a new fundamental limit for sensing when the parameter is not known a priori, moving beyond the standard local-limit paradigm.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Classical global-score hierarchy for a noisy binary model p(x|θ) = Qm ℓ=1(1 + xℓd sin θ)/2, with d = 1/2, wi = 1/n, and θi ∈[0, π]. (a) For fixed n = 8, BFG C /BGCR C →1 as m increases, recovering local Cram´er–Rao behavior. (b) The global Barankin level remains below the Cram´er–Rao scale. (c) For fixed m = 10, refining the sampled domain drives the variance of the maximum-likelihood estimator (MLE) toward BFG C , whereas the global Cram´er–Rao and Barankin levels do not track the attainable variance.

FIG. 2. Bayesian global-score bound for a noisy qubit with Gaussian prior width σp = 5 and decoherence d = 0.5. The local limit ϵ →0 recovers the Van Trees bound. Finite-width kernels retain correlations between distinct parameter values and yield stronger BMSE lower bounds. The black dot marks the optimal kernel width yielding the strongest BMSE lower bound.
